title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Properties -- Bioceramic Sealers
content: Bioceramic sealers are biocompatible materials that use either calcium silicate or calcium phosphate as their main constituent. [21] Mineral trioxide aggregate (MTA) is a new material currently used as a root canal sealer, in addition to its many other uses within the field of endodontics. The formula of MTA is primarily tricalcium silicate with added bismuth oxide, a radiopaque powder for enhanced visualization on radiography. [21] [22] MTA is activated with the introduction of water and produces a highly alkaline mixture with a pH of 12 and beneficial calcium silicate hydrates. These hydrates make MTA an excellent endodontic material as they form on the surface of existing calcium silicate particles and continue to grow inwards, effectively creating a barrier. [21] [22] One major disadvantage of bioceramic sealers is that the enhanced adhesion between the material and the dentin wall makes endodontic retreatment and post-preparation increasingly challenging. [21] [22]

title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Properties -- Bioceramic Materials
content: Bioceramics, particularly MTA, can also be a core filling material. In addition to the aforementioned chemical properties of MTA, it is commonly used as an endodontic material due to its many beneficial characteristics. These include a long set time, a low relative compressive strength, high fracture resistance, and antibacterial and antifungal properties. [26] [27] Furthermore, MTA materials have been consistently proven in the literature to be biocompatible due to their high pH and ability to form hydroxyapatite, an organic substance that promotes bony growth. [28] There are several notable uses of MTA in endodontics, including in the repair of perforations during endodontic treatment, internal root resorption, apexogenesis, and pulp capping procedures. [11] [29]

title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Indications
content: Several key characteristics should be considered when selecting sealers and core obturation materials. Firstly, most sealers are soluble and subject to dissolution when they contact tissue fluids. [32] Additionally, most sealers and core materials irritate the periapical tissues if extruded past the apex, except bioceramic materials, as these materials are biocompatible and rarely act as irritants. [20] [33] Next, sealers differ in their ability to adhere to prepared dentinal walls, with sealers such as ZOE being nonadherent, whereas resin-based sealers are. [9] [34] Lastly, most sealers experience shrinkage upon setting and can create void space between the core material and the dentinal wall, except bioceramic sealers exhibit expansion upon setting. [9] [22] Due to bioceramic materials' enhanced biocompatible and physical properties, they have been indicated and utilized in most cases in modern endodontics. [5] [21] However, using a specific sealer or core material depends entirely on the operator's preference and can be case-specific.

title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Introduction
content: Filling root canals, commonly called obturation, is a critical step in successful treatment. The goals of obturation are twofold: to create an apical seal and fill the root canal without incorporating voids within the filling material. [1] [2] Successful obturation eliminates leakage of contaminants into the channel, such as saliva, bacteria, and periapical tissue fluid, and entraps any residual microorganisms within the canal space. [2] The ability to adequately obturate a root canal is directly related to the preceding step of cleaning and shaping. Cleaning and shaping refer to a root canal's mechanical and chemical preparation to remove organic and inorganic matter. A canal that has been poorly cleaned and shaped results in equally poor obturation and is one of the strongest indicators of treatment failure. [2]

title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Properties -- Glass Ionomer Sealers
content: Clinicians advocating using glass ionomer sealers as an obturation material do so due to their dentin-bonding properties. [15] Dentin bonding results from the reaction between the polyacrylic acid in the glass ionomer and the hydroxyapatite on the dentinal wall and allows for excellent adhesion between the obturation material and the prepared canal wall. [16] Several major advantages arise from the improved adherence, including superior apical sealing, increased resistance to root fracture, and resistance to solubility in tissue fluids. [16] [17] Additional advantages include antimicrobial properties and sustained fluoride release. However, the dentinal walls are hard to reach with primer solutions in the middle and apical thirds, potentially limiting the adhesion of the sealer to the walls. [17] [18] Furthermore, the dentin-bonded glass ionomer sealer creates a homogenous solid material that is difficult to remove during endodontic retreatment. [18]

title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Technique -- Core Obturation Materials
content: The master cone is inserted into the canal to the working length, followed by the spreader, which, if properly selected, should reach 2 mm shorter. The spreader is then rotated and withdrawn from the canal, and the matching gutta-percha cone is inserted into the vacant space. [39] The process is repeated until the operator cannot insert the spreader deeper than the coronal third of the prepared canal. The excess gutta-percha is seared off with a heated instrument and compacted using a root canal plugger. [5] [41] [39]

title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Properties -- ZOE Sealers
content: ZOE-based sealers are the most used sealers in clinical endodontics. They present as a powder-liquid or a 2-paste preparation. [5] Although ex vivo laboratory studies have demonstrated cytotoxic properties in ZOE sealers due to the presence of eugenol, they have proven to be clinically useful in both animal and human studies. [5] [9] Notable properties of ZOE sealers include absorption if extruded into the surrounding tissues and anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial effects. [10] However, ZOE sealers become weak and porous upon setting, making them susceptible to decomposition if tissue fluids leak. [9] [10] Alternatively, other sealers that do not include eugenol are available and, therefore, mitigate any of its potentially deleterious effects. These sealers are aptly referred to as non-eugenol sealers.

title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Technique -- Core Obturation Materials
content: Since there are numerous types of obturation materials, there are several ways to insert them into the root canal, and as a result, this section focuses primarily on the insertion technique of gutta-percha. Gutta-percha has 2 main obturation techniques, cold lateral condensation and warm vertical compaction. [5] [39] The cold lateral condensation technique involves the selection of a master gutta-percha cone, the use of spreaders, and the insertion of secondary gutta-percha cones. [5] [40] The master cone is a gutta-percha cone with a taper and diameter that matches the prepared root canal at the working length. This master cone is measured to the working length and inserted into the canal, where it displays slight resistance to removal, aptly referred to as "tug back" if the correct size cone is selected. [5] After confirming with a radiograph, the canal is irrigated and dried, and sealer is applied to the walls. At this point, a finger spreader or a hand spreader is pre-fit in the canal to reach within 1 to 2 mm short of the working length, and appropriately sized secondary gutta-percha cones are selected that closely match the size of the spreader. [39]

title: Endodontics, Pulp Space Anatomy and Access Cavity of Anterior Teeth -- Clinical Significance
content: TrussAC preserves structural integrity by leaving truss-like segments of the pulp chamber roof intact, creating separate entries to individual canals. This design supports both fracture resistance and long-term restorative success. Guided access techniques—static, dynamic, or hybrid—further improve precision in locating calcified or anatomically complex canals, reduce iatrogenic errors, and minimize unnecessary dentin removal. These methods prove especially valuable in retreatment cases, teeth with pulp chamber obliteration, or when minimally invasive access is preferred.

title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Contraindications
content: There are few true contraindications for sealers and core obturation materials. One major contraindication is an allergy to any component of the material. [35] Allergies to constituent materials are rare but possible in every sealer and core material discussed, and the operator should take care to review all of a patient's possible allergies and be able to respond should a medical emergency arise. Other non-absolute contraindications are case-specific and depend on the treatment goal of the operator.

title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 4 -- Community 2788
content: Amniotomy at delivery is an artificial rupture of the membranes (تمزق الأغشية الاصطناعي), a deliberate obstetric procedure in which a clinician intentionally breaks the amniotic sac to accelerate labor. By creating a controlled opening, amniotomy facilitates the descent of the fetus, enhances the effectiveness of uterine contractions, and allows for more accurate fetal monitoring. The technique is typically employed when labor is progressing slowly, when there is a need to augment contractions, or to expedite delivery for maternal or fetal indications. While amniotomy can shorten the duration of labor and reduce the need for pharmacologic induction, it also carries potential risks such as infection, cord prolapse, or excessive fetal head compression, and therefore is performed after careful assessment of the clinical situation and informed consent. Proper selection of candidates and adherence to aseptic technique are essential to maximize the benefits of this artificial membrane rupture in obstetric care.

title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1769
content: Obstetric therapeutic procedures (الإجراءات العلاجية الخاصة بالتوليد) encompass a range of interventions used to manage labor and delivery, including episiotomy, artificial membrane rupture (تمزق الأغشية الاصطناعي), and vacuum extraction (شفط بالتخلية). Episiotomy is a surgical incision of the perineum performed to enlarge the vaginal opening and facilitate fetal passage. Artificial membrane rupture involves the intentional breaking of the amniotic sac to accelerate labor progression. Vacuum extraction employs a suction device to assist the delivery of the fetus when vaginal birth requires additional traction. Together, these procedures represent key components of obstetric therapeutic care, providing clinicians with options to address various labor complications and to promote safe outcomes for both mother and child.

content: Medical or surgical induction of labor incorporates the procedure known as تمزق الأغشية الاصطناعي (artificial rupture of membranes) as a component intervention. The artificial rupture of membranes is performed directly on the amnion, making the amnion the specific procedural site for this technique.
